lesson.title: Momentum Strategies
lesson.body: Momentum (trend-following in the cross-section) exploits the tendency for recent winners to continue outperforming and recent losers to continue underperforming. The classic Jegadeesh-Titman factor uses 12-month return minus the most recent month (12-1 momentum). Time-series momentum bets that a single asset continues in its recent direction. Both violate the efficient market hypothesis.

rooms.question: The 12-1 momentum factor for stock i at time t is:
rooms.latex: \text{Mom}_{12-1} = R_{t-1\text{ month back}} - R_{t-12\text{ months back}}
answers: 12-month cumulative return, skipping the most recent month
rooms.explanation: 12-1 momentum: buy stocks with highest return over months t-12 to t-2; skip the last month to avoid short-term reversal.

rooms.question: Why is the most recent month excluded from the 12-1 momentum signal?
answers: Short-term reversal: last-month winners often reverse in the next month
Transaction costs are highest for recent trades
rooms.explanation: The 1-month short-term reversal effect (bid-ask bounce, microstructure effects) counteracts momentum at very short horizons.

rooms.question: Cross-sectional momentum strategy is long-short. This means:
answers: Long the top decile of momentum stocks, short the bottom decile
rooms.explanation: Cross-sectional momentum: rank all stocks by past return, buy the top decile (winners), short the bottom decile (losers). The L/S structure is market-neutral.

rooms.question: Order the cross-sectional momentum strategy steps:
items: Compute 12-1 month return for each stock in the universe
rooms.explanation: Classic momentum: compute signal, rank, trade extremes, rebalance. Monthly rebalancing is typical for 12-1 momentum.

rooms.question: Momentum strategies are known to 'crash' because:
answers: In sharp market reversals, winners become losers rapidly (crowded unwind)
Momentum factors have negative expected return
Short-selling is expensive in momentum crashes
rooms.explanation: Momentum crashes (e.g., 2009 rebound) occur when a sudden reversal causes the long (recent winners) to collapse and short (losers) to bounce. Crowded momentum trades amplify losses.

boss.name: The Contrarian
boss.taunt: Yesterday's winners are tomorrow's losers. You are trading stale information.

questions.question: Time-series momentum (TSMOM) for asset i at time t is typically computed as:
answers: Sign of 12-month past return × volatility-scaled position
questions.explanation: Moskowitz, Ooi, Pedersen (2012) TSMOM: sign(r_{12mo}) × (σ_target/σ_i). Go long if past 12-month return is positive, short if negative, scale by target volatility.

questions.question: The autocorrelation of returns at 1-month horizon is typically:
answers: Depends entirely on the asset class
questions.explanation: At 1-month: slight negative autocorrelation (reversal). At 6-12 months: positive (momentum). At 3-5 years: negative again (long-term reversal).

questions.question: Volatility-scaling a momentum position means:
answers: Dividing target dollar exposure by recent volatility σ_i to equalize risk across assets
Increasing position when volatility is high
questions.explanation: Without vol-scaling, high-volatility assets dominate risk. Dividing by σ_i makes each position contribute roughly equal risk, improving diversification and Sharpe ratio.
